title: How a water-quality problem becomes a planning bar
paras: Let's start with how a water-quality problem becomes a planning bar. Nutrient neutrality is not a planning policy that an authority can weigh and set aside. It's the practical consequence of a hard legal duty. And understanding why it can't simply be balanced away is the first thing a client needs to hear. The chain begins with the Conservation of Habitats and Species Regulations 2017 — that's S-I 2017 number 1012, as amended. Those regulations transpose the Habitats and Wild Birds Directives into domestic law, and, despite Brexit, they continue to bind as assimilated law. The Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Act 2023 renamed retained E-U law “assimilated law” from the start of 2024. But it did not sweep the Habitats Regulations away. They remain in force. And the body of E-U case law that interprets them remains influential, subject only to the higher courts’ power to depart from it.

So where a plan or project is likely to have a significant effect on a European site — that's a Special Area of Conservation or a Special Protection Area — either alone or in combination with other plans and projects, and is not directly connected with the site’s conservation management, the competent authority must carry out an appropriate assessment of the implications for the site’s conservation objectives. That is regulation 63. For an ordinary planning application, the competent authority is the local planning authority. On appeal or call-in, it's the Secretary of State or an inspector. The trigger for an appropriate assessment is deliberately low — a likelihood of a significant effect, judged on the precautionary principle. So a real risk that can't be ruled out on the evidence is enough to require one.

The teeth are in the standard that follows. Under regulation 63, subsection 5, the authority may agree to the project only after it has ascertained that it will not adversely affect the integrity of the European site. This is not a balancing exercise. And it is not satisfied by showing that the benefits outweigh the harm. It is a threshold. Unless the authority can be sure, to the standard of no reasonable scientific doubt, that the site’s integrity will not be harmed, it cannot grant permission. Where new residential or overnight development would add to the nutrient load reaching a site already in unfavourable condition because of nutrient enrichment, the authority cannot honestly reach that certainty unless the additional load is cancelled out. That is nutrient neutrality. The applicant must show the development is neutral in its nutrient effect, so that the authority can lawfully conclude no adverse effect on integrity.

Two practical features of the mechanism are worth drawing out. First, the assessment is a two-stage filter. The authority screens for likely significant effects, and only if it cannot rule them out does it move to the full appropriate assessment. But after People Over Wind — which we'll come to — a real nutrient risk cannot be screened out by pointing to mitigation. So in an affected catchment the full assessment is effectively unavoidable for overnight development. Second, although the local planning authority is the competent authority and owns the conclusion, it must consult Natural England as the statutory nature-conservation adviser, and that advice carries very substantial weight. An authority is not bound to follow it, and can depart from it with cogent reasons and its own evidence. But in practice authorities rarely do, because the integrity test demands certainty, and Natural England’s advice is the safest route to defensible certainty. The upshot is that Natural England’s catchment advice, though guidance rather than law, operates on the ground almost as if it were binding.

There is a way past the integrity test, but it is not a way most schemes can use. Regulation 64 allows a project to proceed despite a negative assessment where there are imperative reasons of overriding public interest, there are no alternative solutions, and compensatory measures are secured to protect the overall coherence of the national site network. For nationally significant infrastructure or major strategic development, that argument can occasionally be run. And where the site hosts a priority habitat or species, the reasons that qualify are narrower still, and may require a European Commission-style opinion. For a housing scheme, a holiday-let conversion or a modest rural development, the derogation is effectively a dead end. The “no alternative solutions” limb alone usually defeats it, because a differently-located or differently-mitigated scheme is almost always available. In practice, then, the honest advice is that regulation 64 is not the answer, and neutrality is.

title: The case law that set the approach
paras: Now to the case law that set the approach. The reason the regime bites as hard as it does is a pair of decisions from the Court of Justice in 2018. In People Over Wind and Sweetman against Coillte Teoranta — Case C-323 of 17 — the Court held that mitigation measures cannot be taken into account at the screening stage. You cannot look at a proposal, note that it comes with measures designed to reduce its nutrient impact, and conclude on that basis that there is no likely significant effect and no need for an appropriate assessment. The mitigation has to be assessed within the appropriate assessment, under the precautionary standard. Which means the full regulation 63 exercise is engaged wherever a real nutrient risk exists, and the applicant carries the burden inside it. Before People Over Wind, authorities routinely screened these effects out. After it, they cannot.

The companion cases are the Dutch Nitrogen rulings, Coöperatie Mobilisation for the Environment — Joined Cases C-293 of 17 and C-294 of 17. They struck down a programmatic approach to nitrogen deposition, because it relied on the anticipated future benefits of measures whose effectiveness was not yet established. The lesson carried straight across to nutrients. An authority cannot discharge the integrity test by pointing to future or uncertain improvements. The benefit relied on must be real, secured and effective before it can be counted. That single proposition explains why mitigation for nutrient neutrality has to be locked down in perpetuity and demonstrably additional — anything less is exactly the future, uncertain benefit the Court rejected.

Domestically, the methodology survived its first serious test in R, on the application of Wyatt, against Fareham Borough Council — reported at 2022, E-W-C-A Civ 983. The Court of Appeal rejected a challenge to the lawfulness of Natural England’s precautionary nutrient-budget approach, and to an authority’s reliance on it. It confirmed that building conservative, precautionary assumptions into the budget — and erring on the side of the site — is not irrational, but is what the Directive requires. Wyatt is the answer to the client who insists the assumptions are too cautious. The caution is lawful, and the courts will not second-guess Natural England’s scientific judgement lightly.

The most consequential recent decision is C G Fry and Son Limited against the Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government — reported at 2025, U-K-S-C 35. The Supreme Court held that the regulation 63 duty can be engaged not only at the grant of outline permission, but at the later reserved-matters or discharge-of-conditions stage, where that later approval is what authorises the project to proceed — even where the outline permission predated Natural England’s nutrient advice. In other words, an old permission is not a safe harbour. If the details still have to be approved, the nutrient assessment can catch the scheme on the way through. That is a significant reach, and it is the point most likely to surprise a developer sitting on a phased consent granted years ago. The company nonetheless succeeded on a narrower ground. The site in question, the Somerset Levels and Moors, is a Ramsar site, and Ramsar sites are protected in England as a matter of Government policy, through the Framework, not by the Habitats Regulations as a matter of statute. So the strict regulation 63 duty did not bite in the way argued for a Ramsar-only designation at that stage. That distinction is narrow, and it is being closed. The Planning and Infrastructure Act 2025 places Ramsar sites on the same statutory footing as European sites, so practitioners should not build a strategy on the Ramsar gap surviving. And a note to check: confirm the commencement of the Ramsar provision before relying on it.

Finally, the Ashdown Forest and Wealden line. That's Ashdown Forest Economic Development L-L-P against Wealden District Council — reported at 2015, E-W-C-A Civ 681 — and Wealden District Council against the Secretary of State, reported at 2017, E-W-H-C 351 Admin. This line sits alongside the nutrient cases and matters for the same reason. It established that in-combination effects — there, recreational pressure and traffic-derived air pollution on a protected site — must be assessed, and that a failure to assess them is unlawful. Nutrient neutrality is an in-combination problem par excellence. One house adds little, but every house adds to a load the site cannot absorb. Which is why it is assessed cumulatively, and why “my scheme is tiny” is not an answer.

title: The science, for planners
paras: Now, the science, for planners. You do not need to be an ecologist to advise on this, but you do need to understand the mechanism, because the mitigation follows from it. The protected sites in question are freshwater and estuarine habitats whose condition depends on low nutrient levels. When too much nitrogen or phosphorus reaches them, the water enriches, algae and plant growth explode, and as that material decays it strips oxygen from the water. That's eutrophication. The visible results are algal blooms and choked, deoxygenated water. The ecological result is a site pushed into unfavourable condition, with the species and habitats the designation protects in decline. Because the site is already at or beyond its limit, the law will not tolerate any net addition to the load without compensation.

The two nutrients behave differently, and which one drives a given catchment matters for how the budget is built. Phosphorus is typically the limiting nutrient in rivers, so many of the affected river S-A-Cs are phosphate-led — the River Wye and Lugg, the Somerset Levels and Moors, the River Axe. Nitrogen tends to be the limiting nutrient in coastal and estuarine waters, so those catchments are nitrate-led — the Solent above all, and Stodmarsh in Kent. Some catchments are concerned with both. The practical consequence is that a consultant’s budget for a Wye site is counting kilograms of phosphorus a year, while a Solent budget is counting kilograms of nitrogen. And the mitigation must be denominated in the same currency as the harm.

The nutrients come from two main places. Wastewater from the population a development adds, and agricultural run-off from fertiliser and livestock. Planning can do nothing directly about the farming, which in most catchments is the larger source. But it can control the one thing a new development undeniably adds — new overnight population, whose sewage will, after treatment, still carry nutrients into the catchment. That is why the planning trigger is the addition of people who sleep on the site. A development that adds no overnight population adds no new wastewater load of this kind, and so, in general, adds no nutrient problem. Everything about what is caught, and what is not, flows from that simple point.

title: What actually triggers a requirement
paras: So what actually triggers a requirement? The question on any live case is blunt: does this proposal put more people sleeping in the catchment than before? If it does, and the site drains to an affected protected site, nutrient neutrality is in play. The clearest case is net additional dwellings — housing of any scale, from a strategic urban extension down to a single open-market house or an annexe that creates an independent dwelling. Numbers do not save a scheme. One dwelling in the Solent catchment needs a budget and mitigation just as a hundred do, because the duty is about the integrity of the site and operates cumulatively.

Beyond housing, the net catches overnight visitor accommodation in all its forms, and this is where rural and tourism clients are repeatedly caught out. Holiday lets, glamping pods, cabins, shepherd’s huts, touring and static caravan pitches, hotels and bed-and-breakfast all add overnight population, and all, in principle, need to be neutral. A farmer converting a redundant barn to a holiday let, or putting six glamping pods in a paddock, is proposing exactly the kind of overnight use the regime bites on — however modest, and however “green” the scheme feels. Residential institutions — care homes, student accommodation, hostels — are caught for the same reason, and the occupancy assumptions for these can be higher per unit than for ordinary housing. And a change of use that introduces or increases overnight population is caught even where no building work is involved: a barn to a dwelling, an office to flats, a house to a house in multiple occupation with more bedrooms.

What is generally not caught is development that adds no overnight population: most commercial, employment, retail and industrial floorspace; day-visitor tourism such as a café or a farm shop; agricultural buildings for genuine agricultural use; and like-for-like replacement that does not increase the number of bedrooms or units. The grey areas are real and worth flagging early. A replacement dwelling that is materially larger, or that adds bedrooms, may add occupancy, and so a marginal load. Extensions that create additional bedrooms can, on some authorities’ approaches, add occupancy. Development that mixes day and overnight use needs the overnight element isolated. And the treatment of permitted development and prior-approval conversions to residential — Class Q, Class MA — in affected catchments has been a running sore, because the prior-approval regime and the Habitats duty do not sit together neatly. The safe assumption is that the Habitats Regulations override, so that even a prior approval cannot lawfully be given where an adverse effect cannot be ruled out. On any of these, scope the point rather than assume it away.

The permitted-development interaction deserves its own word, because it trips people up. Prior-approval routes to residential use — agricultural buildings to dwellings under Class Q, commercial premises to dwellings under Class MA — add overnight population just as a full application would, and the Habitats Regulations do not switch off because the route is permitted development. The two regimes pull in opposite directions. Prior approval is a light-touch, narrowly-scoped consent, while regulation 63 is an absolute bar where an adverse effect cannot be ruled out. The safe working assumption is that the Habitats duty prevails, so an authority cannot lawfully give prior approval for a conversion in an affected catchment unless neutrality is demonstrated — which largely defeats the point of the fast-track route, since the applicant still has to produce a budget and secure mitigation. For a rural client eyeing Class Q as a quick win on a barn in the Wye or Somerset catchment, that is unwelcome but important news to deliver early. The interaction is covered in more depth in the Class Q and Class MA guides. The point here is simply that permitted development is not an escape.

The mechanics put the burden squarely on the applicant. The authority is the competent authority and must carry out the appropriate assessment, but it can only conclude “no adverse effect” if the material in front of it — the nutrient budget and the secured mitigation — allows that conclusion. If the applicant does not supply a robust budget and deliverable mitigation, the authority cannot grant, and the application stalls. There is no route by which the authority absorbs the problem. The practical work, and the cost, fall on the applicant, and permission simply does not come until neutrality is demonstrated.

title: The nutrient budget
paras: Let's turn to the nutrient budget. It is the document that decides the case, and a planner should be able to read one critically even without building it. It is calculated using Natural England’s published methodology and the catchment calculators Natural England has issued for each affected catchment. The logic is a before-and-after comparison of the nutrient load leaving the site. On the proposed side, the calculator takes the new overnight population — derived from the number and size of dwellings, or the occupancy of the accommodation, using standard occupancy assumptions — applies an assumed per-person water use and the nutrient concentration of treated wastewater from the works that will serve the site, and adds any change in nutrient export from the land use itself. Because a field taken out of arable production and built on, or a paddock turned to garden, changes what runs off it. On the existing side, it credits the current lawful use of the land. The difference, adjusted, is the development’s net nutrient load, expressed in kilograms of nitrogen or phosphorus a year.

Two features of the calculation matter when you are sense-checking a consultant’s figure. First, it is deliberately precautionary. It builds in a buffer — commonly around twenty per cent — and conservative assumptions throughout, precisely because Wyatt confirms that erring on the side of the site is what the law requires. A client who complains that the numbers are pessimistic is correct, and beside the point. Second, the land-use component can swing the result more than people expect. A development on intensively farmed land can, on paper, reduce the load relative to the farming baseline, while a development on low-nutrient land such as permanent pasture starts from a lower baseline, and so shows a larger net addition. This is counter-intuitive, and it is where budgets are most often argued over. The output you are looking for is a net figure that is zero or negative after mitigation — neutral or better. If the budget shows a positive residual load, the scheme is not neutral and cannot be permitted until it is.

It helps to picture the calculation in words. Take a single new dwelling in a nitrate-led catchment. The calculator assumes a number of occupants, multiplies by an assumed daily water use to get a wastewater volume, and multiplies that by the nitrogen concentration of the effluent leaving the works that will serve the house, to give the nitrogen the new household exports each year. It then adds or subtracts the change in nitrogen running off the land itself — a garden plot carved from an arable field may export less than the field did; a plot taken from rough grazing may export more — and applies the precautionary buffer. The figure that emerges, a modest number of kilograms of nitrogen a year, is what the mitigation must cancel. It sounds trivial, until you realise that securing even a few kilograms a year, in perpetuity, through additional and monitored land management, is neither cheap nor always possible. Which is the whole difficulty of nutrient neutrality in a single sentence.

title: Mitigation — the routes and their catches
paras: Now to mitigation — the routes, and their catches. There are four broad ways to close a nutrient budget, and each has a catch a client needs to hear before they commit.

First, on-site mitigation. The cleanest answer, where the site can take it, is to remove the load on the land you control — creating wetlands or reedbeds that strip nutrients, taking land out of intensive agriculture, or designing sustainable drainage that reduces export. On a large site with spare land this can work, and can be secured through the permission itself. On a small site it usually cannot. A single dwelling or a handful of glamping pods rarely has the land area to create meaningful, monitorable, permanent nutrient reduction, and a token pond will not satisfy an authority applying the precautionary standard. For most modest schemes, on-site mitigation is not available, and the client is pushed to buying credits.

Second, off-site credits — the scheme and the market. Off-site mitigation means securing nutrient reduction somewhere else in the same catchment and tying it to the permission. Two sources exist. Natural England’s Nutrient Mitigation Scheme, launched in March 2023, sells credits from habitat schemes Natural England has funded in a number of catchments. And a private market of habitat banks and land-based schemes sells credits directly. Either way, the mitigation must be additional — it would not have happened anyway — secured in perpetuity, in practice for the lifetime of the development, commonly expressed as eighty to a hundred and twenty-five years, and guaranteed and monitored, because Dutch Nitrogen will not let the authority count an uncertain future benefit. The legal mechanics usually run through a section 106 agreement, or a conservation covenant, that ties the credits to the permission and prevents the development being occupied until the mitigation is in place. The catches are price and availability. Credits are expensive, the price per kilogram has been volatile, and in some catchments and at some times there have simply been no credits to buy. At which point the scheme cannot proceed, however willing the applicant, because there is nothing to secure. This is a real viability and deliverability problem, not a paperwork one, and it should be priced into advice from day one.

Third, land-use change as mitigation. The flip side of the credit market is an opportunity for landowners and farmers. Taking land out of intensive production — converting arable or intensive grassland to low-input grassland, woodland or wetland — reduces the nutrient load reaching the catchment, and that reduction can be sold as mitigation to developers who need it. For a farm business under pressure, a well-located block of land can be worth more as a nutrient-offset asset than as marginal production. What makes such a scheme credible to an authority is exactly what the case law demands: the change must be genuine and additional — not something the land was going to do anyway, and not double-counted against another subsidy or obligation — quantified using Natural England’s methodology, secured in perpetuity by legal agreement, and monitored so the reduction can be shown to be real and maintained. A vague promise to “farm less intensively” will not do. A legally secured, mapped, quantified and managed reduction will. For rural clients this is one of the few genuinely positive angles in the whole subject, and worth raising proactively.

Fourth, strategic and catchment solutions. The largest source of nutrients in most catchments is not new housing but existing wastewater and farming, and the strategic fixes target those. The Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023 placed a statutory duty on water companies to upgrade designated nutrient significant wastewater treatment works — broadly those serving populations of two thousand or more — to meet a demanding nutrient-removal standard by the 1st of April 2030. Where a development drains to such a works, the authority is required, for Habitats purposes, to assume the upgrade will be delivered by the deadline, which reduces the assumed load and can improve a budget. The obvious catch is timing. The duty helps the baseline in 2030, not the applicant who needs to build in 2026, and it does not by itself make a present-day scheme neutral. It changes the arithmetic at the margin. It does not remove the need for mitigation now.

The intended strategic answer is the Nature Restoration Fund and Environmental Delivery Plans under the Planning and Infrastructure Act 2025. The model is straightforward in principle. Rather than each developer sourcing its own scarce credits, a developer pays a levy into a Fund, and Natural England discharges the environmental obligation strategically across the catchment through an Environmental Delivery Plan approved by the Secretary of State, so that the developer’s duty is treated as met. If it works, it removes the credit-scarcity bottleneck and the per-site legal grind. The honest position as at the 18th of July 2026 is that it is not yet available. The Act is in force, the enabling secondary legislation was laid before Parliament on the 18th of June 2026, and Natural England is about to open consultation on the first draft E-D-P, which addresses nutrients. But an E-D-P must be consulted on — for at least twenty-eight days — then approved, before it can be relied on, and none is operational. An applicant who needs a decision this year cannot use it, and must still assemble a conventional budget and secured mitigation. Treat the Fund as the direction of travel, not as a present option, and do not let a client defer a live application on the strength of it.

title: The consultant’s playbook
paras: So, the consultant’s playbook. The first move on any instruction is to establish quickly whether the site is caught. That means two checks. Is the site within, or does it drain to, a catchment covered by Natural England’s nutrient advice? And does the proposal add overnight population? Natural England publishes catchment mapping and the affected authorities keep it on their websites. A site that is outside every affected catchment, or a proposal that adds no overnight use, is clear, and saying so early saves a client a great deal of anxiety and cost. If the site is caught, scope it at the very start, before design is fixed, because mitigation availability and cost may decide whether the scheme is viable at all. And it is far cheaper to find that out at inception than after a year of design work. PlanRef’s constraints map carries the Natural England catchment layer — check the site there as a first-line test of whether it is caught.

Be honest about timescale and cost. Nutrient neutrality routinely adds months to a programme and a material sum to the cost, and on small schemes the mitigation cost can be a decisive proportion of the development value — enough to render a marginal barn conversion or a two-plot infill unviable. Where a scheme depends on buying credits, its deliverability turns on a market that has, at times and in places, had nothing to sell. A client should not exchange contracts or commit to a build programme on the assumption that credits will be there. And where the scheme is phased, remember C G Fry. A later reserved-matters or discharge-of-conditions approval can itself trigger the assessment, so an old outline is not a shield, and cash-flow and mitigation need to be planned across the whole build-out, not just the first parcel.

The issue interacts directly with housing delivery. In the worst-affected areas — the Solent above all — nutrient neutrality stalled tens of thousands of homes for years and blew holes in five-year land supply, which in turn engages the tilted balance and the wider decision-making arithmetic — covered in the Decision-Making area. A planner advising a landowner or authority in an affected catchment should understand that the nutrient bar and the supply position are linked. The bar suppresses delivery, the suppressed delivery weakens supply, and the weakened supply changes how other applications in the district are decided.

For rural and tourism clients — the farm diversification, the holiday accommodation, the barn conversion to overnight use — the specific advice is to raise nutrient neutrality before the client falls in love with the scheme. Because it is precisely these modest, land-based, overnight-use proposals that are caught, that struggle to mitigate on-site, and that can be tipped into unviability by credit costs. The corollary, worth putting to the same clients, is the land-use-change opportunity: a farmer with the wrong land for a holiday let may have exactly the right land to sell as nutrient offset. The common mistakes are predictable and avoidable — assuming a small scheme is too minor to be caught; assuming a “green” or agricultural scheme is exempt; assuming permitted development or a prior approval sidesteps the Habitats duty; assuming the Nature Restoration Fund can be relied on now; and leaving the nutrient question until validation, when it should have shaped the scheme from the outset.

title: Boundaries and related regimes
paras: Let's deal with boundaries and related regimes. Two boundary points keep English practitioners out of trouble. The first is Wales. Nutrient neutrality is a U-K-wide consequence of the Habitats Regulations, but the detail differs across the border. In Wales the adviser is Natural Resources Wales, which set phosphorus targets for the Welsh river S-A-Cs, and the Welsh planning framework and mitigation arrangements are its own. The point bites most obviously on the River Wye, whose catchment straddles the border between Powys and Herefordshire, so a single river system is managed by two regulators under two policy regimes. And the Planning and Infrastructure Act 2025, the Nature Restoration Fund and the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023 upgrade duty are English measures that do not apply in Wales. Do not read across.

The second is that nutrient neutrality is only one of the effects the Habitats Regulations require to be assessed, and it should not be confused with the others. The same regulation 63 duty catches recreational pressure on protected sites — the reason coastal and heathland authorities require Suitable Alternative Natural Greenspace and mitigation contributions for new housing near the New Forest, the Thames Basin Heaths or the Dorset heaths — and air-quality effects from traffic, the issue in the Ashdown Forest line. These are separate assessments with separate mitigation, and a site can be caught by more than one at once. Finally, keep nutrient neutrality distinct from ordinary water-quality and drainage control. A great deal of routine development is conditioned for foul and surface-water drainage, phosphate-stripping package treatment plants, or connection to the mains — sensible controls that manage water quality generally. Those conditions are not nutrient neutrality, and do not, by themselves, discharge the Habitats duty. Nutrient neutrality is the specific requirement, driven by a European-site designation and the integrity test, to demonstrate a net-neutral nutrient effect through secured, additional, perpetual mitigation. Where there is no affected European site, you are in the ordinary drainage world. Where there is, a drainage condition alone will not save the application.

title: The road ahead
paras: Let me end with the road ahead — a clear-eyed word on where this is going, because clients ask, and the temptation to over-promise is strong. The Government has tried more than once to remove nutrient neutrality, and has not managed it. In August 2023 it proposed to disapply the requirement for housing through amendments to what became the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act. The House of Lords rejected those amendments in September 2023, and the requirement remained. That history matters, because it is often misremembered. The duty was not removed, and anyone who tells a client otherwise is wrong.

The current strategy is not to abolish the duty but to discharge it strategically — through the water-company upgrades that improve the baseline by 2030, and through the Nature Restoration Fund and Environmental Delivery Plans that would let developers pay into a pooled solution rather than chase scarce credits. If the Fund launches and the first nutrient E-D-P is approved, the practical burden on individual applicants should ease considerably, and the C G Fry Ramsar gap will by then have been closed by the 2025 Act. But none of that is operational as at the 18th of July 2026. And the sensible course is to advise on the law and the market as they are, treat the reforms as prospects to be tracked rather than tools to be used, and revisit any advice the moment the first E-D-P is made. Because in this subject, more than any other in development management, last year’s note is a liability.
